sentences.text: My name is Louis Hofman.
Yes.
L-O-U-I-S.
Hofman, H-O-F-M-A-N.
And--
I was born in January the 12 of 1912.
I'm today 84.
I was born in-- in Konstantyn a small town not far from the River Bug.
My father's name was-- my Jewish name is [INAUDIBLE].
Mother's name is [INAUDIBLE].
I was born in Konstantyn.
And I was raised there till the Germans destroyed the town and the-- and the people.
What?
Oh, this is near Biala Podlaska.
Biala Podlaska was the state of our town.
Biala Podlaska is a large city.
It was about-- about 15,000, 20,000-- between 15,000 and 20,000 Jews.
No, no-- people. 8,000 Jewish people.
And near it was another city, a big one, [INAUDIBLE].
The river?
This is the River Bug.
Bug-- B-U-G-E.
I had four brothers and four sisters.
Their names, yes.
I'm going from the scratch, from the beginning.
My brother's name was Elie, Elie.
And his wife was [?
Shendel. ?] They had one daughter, married.
She-- the name was Esther Silverman.
Her husband's name was Abraham.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then my-- then the-- the second one was my sister Sara.
Her husband's name was [?
Schloymeh. ?] They had four children.
The old-- the oldest son was [?
Ella. ?] The daughter's name was [?
Chana. ?] And the other two, [?
Moishe ?] and [?
Yankashia. ?] Next was my sister's name, Chaya Rosenbaum.
My brother-in-law's name was Chaim.
The children's name was [?
Chana, ?] the daughter, and [?
Moyshabel, ?] and-- [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] [?
Moyshabel ?] and the other, [INAUDIBLE].
Three more, but I don't remember the name.
Now-- now-- now comes my sister-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Nechama-- Nechama.
It's Goldring.
The husband's name was [?
Yoshia. ?] And the daughter was [?
Connie Etel, ?] Esther, and [?
Tile ?] Goldring.
Now I'm going to my brother after this.
Yankev Hofman.
His wife's name was [?
Shana ?].
It was [?
Shana-- ?] wait, wait, wait.
It was [?
Angela, ?] [?
Wohala, ?] and [INAUDIBLE], and-- and [?
Fimala. ?]
Another-- there comes a sister, Brucha Walecki.
Her husband's name was Eli, Eli.
And the-- she-- they had four children.
It was [?
Chavalla ?], [INAUDIBLE], and [?
Boyshele, ?] and the-- the last one I don't remember.
The last brother's name was Enoch Hofman.
His wife was [?
Sura. ?] They didn't have children, because they both just got married.
My father?
My father's name was Gedale Hofman.
He was a Hasidic Jew from the dynasty [?
Radzim. ?] There was a famous Hasidic culture place in the [INAUDIBLE].
And my father was-- was joined the Hasidic people from [?
Radzim. ?]
Yes, yes.
I still remember the names of the rabbis.
One was [INAUDIBLE].
And [INAUDIBLE].
And the last one was [INAUDIBLE].
In [?
Radzim, ?] Poland.
Not far from Warsaw, from [INAUDIBLE], in that area.
My father used to go a lot of times for Rosh Hashanah there.
But I never went there, because I was about-- small child.
To [?
Radzim? ?] No, no.
My father went.
It was expensive to go there.
It was far.
My father, when he got married, he was Hasidic.
He was, but my grandpa [INAUDIBLE].
But later, my mother was-- my grandpa bought a butcher shop, a kosher butcher shop.
My mother, she knew how to [INAUDIBLE] and how to sell meat to the-- to the housewives.
Then my father was for a short time with them, and then he was-- he went on his own.
He bought a [? car, ?] like in a small town.
He brought to the slaughter house, and they-- there was people and they took-- they took care, and they brought us into the store.
And my mother was [INAUDIBLE] sell the meat.
And he was more or less on the-- on the bookkeeping, what they used to take-- take on credit, and so far.
My father was never selling meat.
My poor mother was working very, very hard in her life-- very hard.
She had to raise eight children and stay in-- in the store.
It was on the main-- main-- on the main street in our town.
In Konstantyn.
The number from the store, I don't remember.
What?
She used to wear a sheitel.
She used to go [INAUDIBLE].
And she used to serve always for poor people.
She-- but mine-- the table was always with somebody, poor people.
Shabbos, and even in the middle of the week, yeah.
My mother, she used to work very hard.
But she prepared everything for Shabbos, a nice Shabbos meal with the children together.
They were singing [INAUDIBLE].
And we were very respectable.
Treated by the parents, and we was very respect the parents.
It was a very close family.
The fact is, a lot of people they were jealous because [INAUDIBLE] Hofman is a nice family.
You know, for-- for Shabbos, they put in a cholent, Friday.
But otherwise, they cooked-- she cooked [INAUDIBLE].
[LAUGHS]
In the bakery, not far away.
I used to bring, because it was not allowed to carry on Shabbos for grown-up people.
The [INAUDIBLE] was not completed.
Then my brother used to go.
After the [INAUDIBLE] [? shul. ?] After we came back from the services.
I would say about 1,500 Jewish people.
No.
It was all kind, like in any other small city, small towns.
It was all kinds.
Oh, there was a nice beautiful synagogue, very-- a nice one.
Very-- And there was a big [INAUDIBLE].
It was a few [INAUDIBLE] went they went to daven.
The rabbis I remember is-- his name was [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah.
I was-- in a few of-- mixed.
They-- [INAUDIBLE] The rabbi was not a teacher for the children.
[SPEAKING IN YIDDISH]
They came in for the-- they went separate, but-- but [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
It was also they went, but separate.
All the holidays was favorite, because was-- everything was-- Pesach was the most popular holiday.
But together, by the Seder, [INAUDIBLE].
Yiddish.
Mamaloshen.
All of them.
Was not so bad, but was no good.
They was-- one thing of the Jews, Yiddish, this and that.
But it was not too bad.
Anti-Semitic was at that time also.
In Poland.
In my-- in my hometown even, yeah.
Very small.
One bedroom, one living room, and one kitchen.
We used to sleep two, three people in one bed.
Not far away.
About half a block.
It was a small town.
I went to the Polish school.
Seven grades.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE], Polish [INAUDIBLE] School, and then I went to cheder.
And I went to a private teacher for writing and reading Yiddish.
And the [INAUDIBLE]-- And this was--
No.
We had to help the parents to make a living.
We couldn't do any other things.
We got this-- after the bar mitzvah, I had to help out already.
It was a-- a very poor bar mitzvah.
I went to the [INAUDIBLE] to the Sefer Torah, to make the barucha, and say the haftarah.
And my father, rest in peace, he brought a bottle of whiskey and a piece of cake and we drank l'chaim.
And that's it.
This was in '25, 1925, 13-- 13 years.
And 13 years and one day, you got the bar mitzvah.
I worked for [INAUDIBLE].
I helped my parents to make a living.
Till what age?
Then I was-- in 1936, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and '37.
I opened up-- as a young boy, I opened up a store-- a hardware store, because that time was very bad for the kosher butcher stores.
Was a lady in the government from Poland by the name Mrs.
Pristor.
Yeah, Pristor.
P-- I'll-- I'll-- you want [INAUDIBLE]?
Pristor, P-R-I-S-T-O-R.
Pristor.
She was in the-- in the senate.
And she was pushing to forbid the Jewish people to kill for kosher.
It was very anti-Semitic.
And she passed a law for Jewish people so much.
And that's it.
[INAUDIBLE] kosher meat, you couldn't have trafe together.
It was a [INAUDIBLE] small town, was four kosher butchers.
Then they had to close up, because only one was kosher.
And this was a-- a problem, a big, big problem.
It was bad already.
Huh?
Was a lot of times, was very, very short.
But they couldn't help it.
They covered up with poultry.
Poultry was not included.
Only cows, lambs, calves, and so forth.
I was reading the Jewish newspapers, the-- the [INAUDIBLE], then there was [INAUDIBLE], you know, and another couple.
But I was reading the [INAUDIBLE].
I worked like a horse.
That was my hobby in my life.
I was-- [LAUGHS] it's a good question.
I was not thinking good.
To start it good, before I'm doing.
But I went in blind, and I did it.
And I succeeded.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I took a chance.
It was good.
Till the Germans came in, and they took away everything for the-- the Polish people come in.
I was forced to open up the store, and they [INAUDIBLE].
I had all kinds of hardware, for-- for horse and wagons, for the wagons, material.
And for houses, locks, for windows the hinges.
And a lot of things.
Nail.
And-- it's very hard to explain everything, but I-- like in a small town, the hardware like in any other small towns.
It was another store.
Yes, my brother's daughter, Esther Silverman.
She was working with me.
She was in the 20s.
Before the war?
Five days.
Saturday was closed, and Sunday was not allowed to open up by the Polish law.
Till '39.
In September, the war came in, and took [? more than ?] two weeks, and the Germans came in.
I was in the-- on the waiting list to be called in.
But they-- before they looked around, it was already after the war.
The Germans arrived.
I mean, after the-- after the Polish army was fighting, it was finished.
It took two weeks, too, probably.
The purpose was I had at that time-- I was-- went through a sickness or something.
I don't remember what kind.
Not such a serious, but they took me in-- [? we'll take him ?] in reserve.
Yes.
What-- what did-- what did you do in the reserves?
Did you have to practice something?
Did you have to go for training?
If they-- if they would call me in, they would train me to use weapons.
But unfortunately, it was already-- two weeks was finished.
The Polish army couldn't-- couldn't do anything.
They gave up.
I didn't go in.
They didn't call me.
Yes.
I think this was in 1933.
Yeah.
Oh, there was a-- a few organizations.
I belonged to the [INAUDIBLE].
And that was a lot of [INAUDIBLE].
Like in-- like in every small town in Europe, it was [INAUDIBLE].
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] let me just remind myself.
There was the-- like in any other state.
He was very proud of me.
They were in the meat business.
And they-- they struggled very bad, because account of the-- of the non-kosher law came out.
Every Polish-- maybe not every, but a lot of them, they already mentioned Hitler's name.
Hitler's going to come on you.
When he has something, Hitler's gonna come.
And we started to suffer a lot already when Hitler-- when Hitler became in power.
Polish governments kept together with a relationship.
And anti-Semitic was jumping, growing from the ground, in moments everything.
What happened at that time?
All right.
Go ahead.
Don't press me too much politically.
I was not.
OK.
